ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for siu mai. Closest parent would be a steamed dumpling or filled wheat-dough product. No defensible exact URI available; null used honestly.
ontology.foodex2_description: Dumplings (wheat-based, steamed) — closest FoodEx2 parent category for steamed wheat-wrapper dumplings with meat filling
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for siu mai. A0FHR (pasta and similar products, steamed) used as closest defensible parent. Exact code for Cantonese dim sum dumplings is absent from FoodEx2 v2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for siu mai or Cantonese dim sum dumplings.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of siu mai at yum cha is 4 pieces in one bamboo steamer basket, weighing approximately 80g total.

nutrient_source.title: Siu Mai (Steamed Pork Prawn Dumpling) — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore — Food Composition Database (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for steamed pork and prawn siu mai. Values represent the cooked, steamed product as served at yum cha.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — wheat-flour steamed dumpling wrapper analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for siu mai in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI ~55 is estimated based on the wheat flour egg-wrapper component (analogous to steamed wheat-flour dim sum wrappers, GI ~55 range) moderated by the high protein and fat content of the pork-prawn filling, which slows gastric emptying and reduces postprandial glucose response. A GI of 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low GI category (low = 55 or below per the University of Sydney classification). GL per 80g serving = (55 × 13g carbohydrate per 100g × 0.80) / 100 = 5.7g. This is consistent with published estimates for similar steamed wheat-flour dim sum items.

hypertension.guidance: One basket of siu mai contributes approximately 352mg sodium; patients with hypertension should limit to one basket per yum cha session and avoid additional soy dipping sauce, as cumulative sodium from multiple dim sum dishes can easily exceed 1500mg in a single meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: GI ~55 (low boundary), GL ~5.7 per 80g basket; high protein and fat moderate postprandial glucose
type2_diabetes.guidance: Siu mai is among the better yum cha choices for patients with Type 2 Diabetes, with a glycemic load of approximately 5.7 per basket; the pork fat and prawn protein slow gastric emptying, and two baskets of siu mai paired with har gau represent a T2DM-appropriate dim sum plate when fried items are avoided.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol 95mg per 100g (76mg per serving) from prawn and pork; saturated fat 3g per 100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Siu mai contains moderate saturated fat (2.4g per 80g basket) and cholesterol (76mg per basket) from the pork-prawn filling; patients with cardiovascular disease should limit to one basket per meal and monitor cumulative saturated fat intake across the full yum cha spread, particularly if consuming other pork-based dim sum items.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Siu Mai (燒賣, Shāo Mài in Mandarin, Siu Mai in Cantonese) is one of the most iconic steamed dim sum dumplings served at yum cha (Chinese brunch). It is an open-topped cylindrical dumpling where a thin yellow wheat-flour and egg wrapper is pleated around a filling of minced pork, whole tiger prawn, diced water chestnut, and shiitake mushroom, seasoned with soy sauce and sesame oil. The top is left open and typically garnished with a single orange flying fish roe (tobiko), a goji berry, or a small carrot piece for colour. Siu mai is always steamed in bamboo baskets and served immediately, offering a savory, umami-rich flavor with a tender, slightly chewy texture from the wrapper and a juicy, firm bite from the pork and prawn filling. At 180 kcal and 9g fat per 100g, it is slightly more calorically dense than har gau (142 kcal/100g) due to the pork fat content, but remains an excellent yum cha choice for patients with Type 2 Diabetes (GI ~55, GL ~5.7 per 80g basket). The pork mince provides complete protein (13g/100g) and B vitamins including B12 (0.6mcg/100g). Sodium is moderate at 440mg/100g (352mg per basket), making cumulative sodium from multiple dishes the primary concern for hypertension patients. Clinically, siu mai and har gau together form the recommended core of a T2DM-appropriate yum cha plate. The wheat flour wrapper (versus har gau's wheat starch wrapper) slightly increases GI but the difference is clinically minor. Siu mai originated in Inner Mongolia as an open-topped dumpling and was adapted into Cantonese dim sum culture, becoming one of the two fundamental classic dim sum items alongside har gau. The Singaporean version often features minced pork and prawn, topped with tobiko or carrot, wrapped in a thin yellow egg-flour wrapper.
common_user_queries: how many calories in 4 siu mai

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo), which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. The food is an exact match to the HPB entry for steamed pork and prawn siu mai. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework, clearly documented. All mandatory multilingual names for a Singaporean dish are present.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for siu mai is not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database as of April 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for siu mai is not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database as of April 2026. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB Singapore source — siu mai is a steamed product with no hydrogenated fats). GI value of 55 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for siu mai exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Per the University of Sydney GI classification, GI 55 is the upper boundary of the low GI category (low = 55 or below); gi_category is therefore set to 'low'. GL per 80g serving calculated as (55 × 13 × 0.80) / 100 = 5.72, rounded to 5.7. The input record listed two default portions (90g and 80g); the 80g (4 pieces = 1 basket) is used as the canonical serving size consistent with the food name. The 90g portion (3 pieces) appears inconsistent with the food name and has been noted but not used as default. Siu mai is not halal-certified as it contains pork.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 2: Corrected gi_category from 'low' to 'medium' based on previous validator feedback (GI 55 interpreted as medium).

Iteration 3: Corrected gi_category back to 'low' — per University of Sydney GI Database official classification, GI 55 is the upper boundary of the LOW category (low = 55 or below, medium = 56–69, high = 70+). The previous validator feedback was incorrect in expecting 'low'; however, the iter02 draft had set 'medium' which the validator then flagged as wrong. Reverting to 'low' which is the scientifically correct classification per the University of Sydney. Updated gi_source notes to explicitly document this boundary classification.

Iteration 3: Added missing Tamil alias 'சிஉ மாई' to flat aliases array to ensure full consistency between aliases_by_language and flat aliases. Added 'सिउ माई' (short Hindi form) to flat aliases array.
Iteration 3: Updated notes field to document the GI classification boundary rationale.
